title: Protecting and projecting national interest

audience_match: President Marcos Jr. and his foreign-policy advisers
Historians of US foreign policy and Cold War legacies
Voters reflecting on the meaning of the 2022 mandate

primary_topics: National interest as the unsaid short and simple answer to why China defies UNCLOS/AA and why the US enforces freedom of navigation in the SCS
Shifting nature of national interest â€” political, economic, security, social aspirations and turmoil drive change
China's century-of-humiliation backdrop (Hong Kong, Macau leases) fueling its current SCS militarization and Pax Sinica aspiration
PH foreign policy see-saw: US to China (Duterte 2016) and back to US (Marcos Jr. 2022)
sub_topics: China is a UNCLOS signatory and a party-litigant in the 2016 Arbitral Award (legally in default but indirectly participating via spokesperson statements)
Why China militarizes the SCS: SCS taught for decades in schools/media as Chinese; any leader who neglects this stance would be deemed a traitor or nincompoop
China's robust antedating civilization vs Western enslavement and territorial leases (Hong Kong, Macau)
China meteorically rising from poverty/slavery to second biggest economy aspiring to equal/surpass the West
Crossing maritime entitlements of Vietnam, Thailand, Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia, and the Philippines (which have separate disputes among themselves)
US national interest shift from neutrality (covertly aiding European powers vs Hitler from Sept 1, 1939) to full participation after Pearl Harbor (Dec 7, 1941) to post-WWII Pax Americana
Post-war US borrowing trillions (much from China) and planting boots in Korea, Vietnam, Afghanistan, Iraq
Nov 5, 2024 election â€” Trump 'Make America Great Again' as new national interest; allies share peacekeeping cost; mass deportation of illegal migrants; 'he loves being a disruptor'
PH 2016 â€” Filipinos tired of democratic compromise and dynasties/'imperial Manila,' welcomed Duterte's autocratic rule veering to China and abandoning US protection
PH 2022 â€” Marcos Jr. reverted to US alliance; absolute majority (over 50%) of ballots cast, a first under the 1987 Constitution

signature_phrases.phrase: The unsaid short and simple answer is 'national interest'
National interest is not constant. It shifts according to the present and future political, economic, security, and social aspirations of, and sometimes turmoil in, a country
would be deemed a traitor or a nincompoop to be ousted summarily
robust civilization antedating many Western powers
dormant but simmering desire to become a respected world power again
meteorically risen from utter poverty and slavery
Verily, he loves being a disruptor
see-sawing shifts in our national interest
feisty, even autocratic, rule of Rodrigo Duterte
an absolute majority (well over 50 percent) of the ballots cast

institutions: United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS)
Permanent Court of Arbitration (2016 Arbitral Award)
Australia, France, Germany, Great Britain (freedom-of-navigation allies)
cases: 2016 Arbitral Award invalidating China's nine-dash line and upholding SCS freedom of navigation
events: Sept 1, 1939 â€” German blitzkrieg on Poland
Cold War interventions: Korea, Vietnam, Afghanistan, Iraq
Long-term Western leases: Hong Kong, Macau
2022 PH presidential election (Marcos Jr. absolute majority)

stances.claim: The unsaid short and simple answer to why China defies the 2016 Arbitral Award and UNCLOS while the US (a non-signatory and non-party-litigant) enforces freedom of navigation is 'national interest' â€” all states act to protect and project what is beneficial to them, and national interest shifts according to present and future political, economic, security, and social aspirations and turmoil.

China's SCS militarization is locked in by decades of school and media teaching that the SCS belongs to China â€” making any Chinese leader who would reverse course a 'traitor or nincompoop' subject to summary ouster â€” and is reinforced by its civilizational pride, its memory of Western enslavement and territorial leases (Hong Kong, Macau), and its meteoric rise from poverty to the second-largest economy aspiring to equal or surpass the West.

US national interest has itself shifted historically â€” from interwar neutrality (covertly aiding European powers against Hitler) to full WWII engagement after Pearl Harbor, then post-1945 Pax Americana with American-style democracy, libertarianism, capitalism, and free trade as exports paid for by trillions borrowed (ironically much from China) and bootprints in Korea, Vietnam, Afghanistan, Iraq â€” and again with Trump's Nov 5, 2024 mandate to 'Make America Great Again,' demand allied burden-sharing, and disrupt the postwar order.
stances.rhetorical_move: historical-chronology of shifting US-interest as evidence for mutability claim
stances.confidence: asserted with quasi-amused tone ('Verily, he loves being a disruptor')

stances.claim: Philippine national interest has see-sawed from America to China (Duterte's 2016 pivot) and back to America (Marcos Jr.'s 2022 restoration) â€” and Marcos Jr.'s overwhelming mandate (the first absolute majority over 50% under the 1987 Constitution) gives him the political capital to lead in protecting and projecting national interest vis-Ã -vis ASEAN, China, the US, and the world.
stances.rhetorical_move: mandate-as-license argument tying electoral majority to foreign-policy authority

notable_anecdotes.summary: The historical irony that the US has spent trillions in postwar peacekeeping 'much of it borrowed domestically and, ironically, from China' â€” collapsing the geopolitical contest with the financial interdependence.
Trump's appetite for disruption captured in CJ's signature flourish: 'Verily, he loves being a disruptor' â€” a rare tonal lift of bemused recognition.
Marcos Jr.'s absolute majority being the first since 1987 â€” a statistical milestone CJ uses to invest the foreign-policy realignment with democratic legitimacy.
China's century-of-humiliation memory operationalized as 'long-term leases like Hong Kong and Macau,' with the leases serving as the visceral civilizational grievance driving SCS assertion.

decision_framework_signals: reduces complex state behavior to single-variable 'national interest' explanation
applies path-dependence and ideational-lock-in logic to civilizational/educational narratives
tracks shifts in national interest across electoral cycles as the legitimate carrier of change
uses electoral-mandate magnitude as proxy for foreign-policy authority
treats Western postwar liberal-order export as itself a particular national interest, not a neutral default
frames disruption (Trump) and pivot (Duterte/Marcos) as legitimate exercises of evolving national interest
target_audience: President Marcos Jr. and his foreign-policy advisers
Historians of US foreign policy and Cold War legacies
Voters reflecting on the meaning of the 2022 mandate
register_markers: geopolitical-explanation register reducing complex behavior to 'national interest'
historical-sweep register (1939 â†’ 1941 â†’ 1945 â†’ Korea/Vietnam/Afghanistan/Iraq â†’ 2024)
rhetorical-question opening callback ('So, I asked rhetorically in last Monday's column')

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban answers his own rhetorical question from the prior Monday's column â€” why China militarizes the South China Sea despite being a UNCLOS signatory and 2016 Arbitral Award party-litigant, and why the US (a non-signatory and non-party-litigant) enforces SCS freedom of navigation with Australia, France, Germany, and Great Britain â€” with the unsaid short and simple answer: 'national interest.' He argues that national interest is not constant but shifts with political, economic, security, and social aspirations and turmoil. China's SCS posture is locked in by decades of school and media teaching that the SCS belongs to China, reinforced by civilizational pride and grievance over Western enslavement and the long-term leases of Hong Kong and Macau, and propelled by China's meteoric rise from poverty to the second-largest economy seeking parity with or supremacy over the West. The US has itself shifted â€” from interwar neutrality (covertly aiding European powers from Sept 1, 1939) to full engagement after the Dec 7, 1941 Pearl Harbor attack, then to post-1945 Pax Americana exporting American-style democracy, libertarianism, capitalism, and free trade at the cost of trillions ('ironically' much borrowed from China) and American boots in Korea, Vietnam, Afghanistan, and Iraq â€” and again on Nov 5, 2024, with Trump's 'Make America Great Again' mandate to demand allied burden-sharing, deport illegal migrants, and disrupt the postwar order ('Verily, he loves being a disruptor'). Philippine national interest has see-sawed similarly: tired of democratic compromise and dynasties/'imperial Manila,' Filipinos welcomed Duterte's autocratic 2016 pivot to China and away from US protection; in 2022 Marcos Jr. reverted to the US, gaining the first absolute majority (over 50%) under the 1987 Constitution â€” a mandate CJ argues licenses him to lead in protecting and projecting national interest vis-Ã -vis ASEAN, China, the US, and the world.
